segments.text: Katie, is it?
Yes.
Welcome.
We're just going to do a little introduction quickly just for everyone watching.
Yeah.
We'll kick on, get underway.
Okay.
All right. Good afternoon, everyone.
We
are back with the Social Services and Community Select Committee, and we've got two
sub-committees today.
So I'm chairing one sub-committee.
And so I'm Joseph Mundy, the Member of Parliament for Southland and the chair of
the committee. And we'll just go quickly around who else is here.
Hi, I'm Laura McClure. I'm the Act list MP based down in
Christchurch.
Kia ora. I'm Helen White. I'm the MP for Mount Albert.
I've also got a couple of portfolios, the Family and Sexual
Violence Prevention portfolio and Community and Voluntary.
I'm going to be replaced at some point in this committee by
Priyanka Ramakrishnan, who has the Disability portfolio.
Kia ora. My name is Ricardo Menéndez March.
I'm a Green Party MP based in Mount Albert, and I do Social
Development and Employment, as well as Immigration and a few
other portfolios for the Greens.
Welcome, Katie. Thanks for having you this afternoon.
Thanks for coming in to make a submission. The time is yours when you're ready.
Thank you. And thanks for the opportunity.
So to introduce myself, I'm Katie Townsend.
I'm currently a student at Victoria University of
Wellington,
doing political science and public policy.
And
disability is an interest of mine because I am disabled
myself and have several family
friends
in Wellington and Christchurch.
Great. Thanks.
So the first concern is regarding Clause 8
and the family first.
So,
it brings the assumption that
families have
the capability and resources to be able to
provide care
for the disabled member,
which is not always the case.
And
also,
in a lot of cases, it is unpaid.
Mm-hmm.
And those who do get paid, it is usually because they're
eligible for the Supportive Living Payment.
So, some of these
considerations before
providing care, first of all is
willingness. I do understand that family does
have the natural responsibility to provide care,
but that may not always be the case.
It could be because they may not have the financial
resources available, or they may not have the
skills,
especially in cases where the disabled person has complex needs.
And also other commitments, such as other family
members,
and
also
work and,
in some cases, say, the family member may live in
another part of the country or the world.
So,
my recommendation is,
one would be
a carer capacity assessment to make sure
that they do have that capacity to
provide that quality care.
My second concern is regarding Clause 11,
and how the majority of that framework
is passed through secondary legislation,
which, from my understanding,
does not go under the same public debate and
scrutiny as primary legislation.
But it's also,
you have to rely,
because you have to decide
what--
had to put trust on that minister
to serve in
the community's best interests,
and without any safeguards in place-
Yeah
those decisions can be made based on government priorities
instead of
the interest in the community.
The third one is consultation.
Yes, there may be some consultation, but for a
framework, it probably would need to be a lot more comprehensive
to make sure,
especially the ministers and the people creating that bill,
understand those real-world impacts,
and make sure
that the objectives
of the disability community and the government align
with each other.
And the final concern is with the bill itself.
First of all, it relies, that's
from my own analysis, it's quite heavily focused on family
circumstances instead of the support needs of the person.
And another one is,
it's quite looked at from a...
If we're talking about disability support services, it focuses
on
care support. And despite when disability support services
provide a lot of other things,
such as housing, transport,
child development services that I have used previously,
education, and
employment services.
And I do believe
if we're doing a framework,
it has to cover everything,
especially in the primary legislation.
And the last one,
it's quite focused on from a social development
point of view.
When you actually--
Because it talks a little bit more about funding allocation-
Mm
... resource allocation,
but disability affects a lot of different things, not just social
development. It affects health, education, employment,
housing and transport, and other sectors.
So,
we do need to have a--
It shouldn't be based on a single department.
It should be more of a cross-agency approach
to make sure it
covers all areas.
Great. Thanks, Cody. I'd love to ask you questions, but we don't have time.
But thank you very much-
Yeah. It's all good
... for your presentation this afternoon. Appreciate it.
Thank you.
Thank you very much.
Got it. Thank you.
All right, next submitter is Rebecca, I think also in person.
Welcome.
Kia ora.
Kia ora.
All right. We've got five minutes with you this evening as well, so time is yours-
... when you are ready.
Cool. Thank you.
Kia ora. My name is Rebecca Collett.
I'm here as a parent of a teenager with high needs who lives at
home.
I'm not very good with eye contact, however, it's important for me to be here today
to look you-
No, thank you
... in the eye.
To say this: please don't proceed with this bill.
Don't find yourselves on the wrong side of history.
This bill will negatively impact yet another generation
of disabled people and their families in
Aotearoa. The fact that this bill has made it to
this stage shows that we have learnt nothing
of how to treat disabled people from the Royal
Commission of Inquiry into Abuse and State Care.
It's a spit on the unmarked graves of disabled people that lived
and died in institutions, and it's the ultimate betrayal, I
think, to the survivors who gave evidence in good faith and trusted in
the process, hopeful that it would help shape a
better future for future generations of disabled people.
We said never again, yet here we are disregarding disabled people's
human rights.
Institutions in the past hid people away, denying
access to education, healthcare, employment, community,
and it feels like this is happening again.
It's same but different. This time it's happening in slow motion
and in full view of all of us.
Institutions of the past were four walls made of brick and mortar.
People were placed far away from family and community.
Now, with supports being stripped back, disabled
people are effectively being controlled and isolated in their own
homes with no free will as to who cares for them, what
services they engage with, and how they live their lives.
I today heard the Minister of Disability saying that this bill will not
change day-to-day life for disabled people and their families.
Funding will stay the same and new means testing will not be
introduced. However, my opinion is that this bill sets the
rules for the future. So it might not change today, but what about
tomorrow?
Making it easier to limit access, cap support, and
remove ability for recourse when the wheels fall off.
The current government might have
the ability to stop the rollout of enabling good lives, to lessen
subsidies for total mobility scheme, to tinker with the
purchasing guidelines of individualized funding,
but the UNCRPD still exists, and the
government has an obligation to support disabled people
to live their lives without barriers.
And families, like my own, who have long
plugged up the gaps in an unsupportive system,
forsaking their own physical and mental wellbeing, will now be
expected to financially support their disabled loved ones on top
of everything else, no matter what their capacity is.
This government continues to punch down on vulnerable
communities to save a buck.
Of all the changes that they can make to disabled people's lives, how is
this the one that they chose to make?
The snowball effect this bill will have for disabled people and their families will
be immense.
For me, if we can't afford to pay a carer for my daughter, I'll have to give up
my own employment, which means I can't provide for my family,
which will detrimentally impact my own
self-worth and mental health, which means I can't be
the mum I want to be to my kids.
This bill should not proceed, and if it must, you should at least extend
the hearing process so you can hear from more people around the
country
who will be impacted by this bill. 19 days for submissions
and five minutes per oral submission for a select
few is negligent, and this is very
anxiety--
It's given me anxiety, but
you have an obligation to listen to as many of the very
people that this bill will impact.
So committee members, please do the right thing,
not the expected thing. Ka mua, ka muri.
Let the past guide how we move forward.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Ooh.
Um
Made it under five.
Well done.
Nice.
Well done. Rebecca. Just a really quick question. Thank you.
What do you see could be some of the worsening outcomes
for disabled people under the bill as it
stands?
I really am concerned about health.
Mental health. I'm concerned
about carers
who are already at their limit that are going to have to
take on more responsibilities, and this will
detrimentally impact our mental health across the board.
Sure. Thank you.
People are already struggling,
and
looking for support in the wrong places. So, yeah, that's my concern.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Oh, thanks, Rebecca. Thanks for coming in and making submissions. I think.
Thank you.
Our next submitter is Sienna via Zoom.
Can you hear me?
We can, yes. Thank you.
Yes.
Oh, welcome, Sienna. So you have five minutes this afternoon.
The time is yours when you're ready.
Oh, thank you. Tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you for
giving me the opportunity to speak today.
I would like to acknowledge the people that have spoke before me.
My name is Sienna, and I'm a mother of a 26-year-old
daughter,
with significant disabilities and complex medical needs
My daughter lives at home and needs full-time support every
day. For the last 26 years, our family has organized our
lives around her care.
With one parent have always stayed at home and the other went to work.
As a result, we have lived on one income for the past 26
years.
This has meant major sacrifices in income, career opportunities,
retirement saving, financial security, along with just the
daily emotional and physical demand of care.
And those challenges are shared by many New Zealand families
across, that provide support for their
family members with disabilities.
We do have a duty of care as families,
but the government also has the responsibility to
support their citizens with disabilities,
and uphold their mana.
This bill is trembling on everybody's mana.
I go to work every day. I don't ask for a lot, but this bill is
just...
It's awful.
Family support should not be substituted for government
support. As a citizen, people with
disabilities should be supported.
My biggest concern about the Disability Support Service Bill,
it seems to be focused more on controls,
reassessment,
management
of financial, instead of the well-being of the people that it's
meant to be providing for.
I'm concerned that the assessment and
reassessment will create even more
uncertainties,
especially for the people with
special needs and their families.
My daughter's special needs are well-documented, so a new assessment
is not going to change what had happened for the last 26
years.
It's just going to create an environment of distrust,
and it's going to create anxiety and insecurity, and it just impacts
the well-being of our whanau. What I
would like to see is a system that is built with
working with us as families with the disability support services,
that can actually look at the real picture and the impact on
families and people with special
needs. Not what has happened
today. This bill was put together so the government can save
money,
and it has not taken our needs. It's not listened to us,
and it is true. The consultation, five
minutes for each speaker.
You are dealing with a group of people that are already tired.
We already give a lot.
Our children don't grow up and leave
and have their own lives and their own careers.
I don't come home from work
and don't have anything to do. I have another job that I come home
to, and that's what it's been like for 26 years.
We want a greater recognition of the family
carers and the contribution that we actually put in.
The fact is that we have to fight for everything.
It's
dehumanizing.
It just makes you feel like you're the worst parent, even though you
really do try as a parent. I go and work full time,
and I come home and I work full time.
And this bill really--
It's awful.
At the heart of it, the bill should ensure that people with disability can live
with dignity, security, choices, and fully participate
in society.
This is not what this bill going to create.
We're not asking for special treatment.
We are asking for fairness, stability, and recognition.
And the government has an ongoing
responsibility for its citizens,
regardless if they are able to work
or not.
My child is a citizen of this country.
I pay my taxes. I'm not actually asking for a lot.
Just please don't change the system so much.
Thank you.
Thanks, Janice. Thanks for making submission.
And look, I'll just make the comment that in my view, it's not doing that.
But we don't have time to go on that.
But can I just make one other comment, because you're the second person to say
this, this afternoon, about five minutes.
The committee's made a decision to give five minutes to every submitter.
There's a lot of people who want to make submissions, so we're trying to hear from
as many as possible, and so it's quite normal for committees to give five minutes
for submitters on bills.
But-
But thanks for making submissions this afternoon
Can I just make a point here?
Yeah, go for it.
There is a limited brief on this. We have to have it back
by the 13th of August, and that's a decision that's been taken by the
minister. So we are in a position that is truncated.
I know-
Oh, but Helen, we as a committee, we decide how much time we set aside-
We couldn't possibly-
And
we're doing it
... we couldn't possibly-
We could
... sit out-
We're doing it
... because we have to have it back because of what the government-
Helen, we could sit three weekends if we wanted to.
So-
It's really important submitters know why we are doing this in
such a rushed fashion.
It's normal, Helen, for committees to do five-minute hearings.
Mr. Chair, could you hand the time, including your time, back to
submitters? Because I don't think this is our time to bicker.
It's their time.
It was not bickering, Ricardo.
I mean, you're literally bickering with presenters.
I think it's been balanced. I think it's been, but yeah
All right. Now, I'm sorry, Luciana, that was not about you.
That was a discussion between committee members with a bit of a political
difference on things. But thanks for making a submission this afternoon.
We appreciate it.
Thank you.
Our next submitter is Claire. Madam President.
Good afternoon. Welcome. Thanks, Claire. All right.
We've got five minutes with you when you're ready. Fire away.
Cool. Thank you.
I just wanted to focus on a few things.
I know a lot of people have talked about the impact this has on people's
lives.
Obviously, I've got disabilities
myself, so I'm a bit invested in this, but I am also somebody who
implements a lot of legislation, and I'm quite concerned with this
particular piece.
Mm-hmm.
The key problems is that it appears to be yet another
panic piece, where it's solely aimed
at shutting down
a specific group of society seeking a remedy
that has been breached in some way, whether it be environmental or whether it be
offshore and seabed with-- and now
we're looking at employment law
based on human rights.
It's ill-considered, and the
result of this would be a declaration
of inconsistency coming through, because it is breaching--
Well, breaching, but it is inconsistent with both
our ratification of the
UNCRDP and
also the Human Rights Act.
And I do also have concerns over the
way it was formed when you consider things like
Section 7.68 of the Cabinet Manual,
Section 5 of the Standing Orders, which
suggest that
whoever creates a bill should be considering things like
the Bill of Rights and really consider good reasons as
to why you
would create
a conflict with that. And right now, the only real conflict
is we have no plan-- the justification, sorry, is that we have no
plan, and we think it's going to cost too much.
So we're going to basically
remove our accountability
to the New Zealand public
by creating this conflict in the Act.
It is also worrying because it childifies people with disabilities.
A child is the responsibility of their family until
they become an adult.
And a child, if they can't be cared for,
their family then becomes the responsibility of the state to step in.
Excuse me. That is essentially what you're
doing with people with disabilities.
You are saying that you will only step in as the
state if the family can't support them, and that puts them on a similar
level as a child.
It also impacts everybody, regardless of disability,
because
this would look at people with age, their working history, and all other
parts of society. It's quite a scattergun piece.
What I think should happen is that we
kill this bill before the election. There are some reasons for that.
And
it bases around the fact that we really need a good trunk of legislation.
A really good piece of legislation is something that it can
endure multiple parties, multiple terms,
multiple philosophies because those principles are really solid.
If you think of the Employment Relations Act that came in in 2000, it's
got a good trunk. You've trimmed around the outside now and again, but
those trunk of principles have remained, and that's what I would like to
see,
this bill be changed to include these really good principles.
I'd also recommend that we look at
things from a horizontal viewpoint rather
than silos. So we've got a disability strategy at the
moment, but it's all about health.
We haven't looked at things like
the intersectionality
between health, education, social services, justice,
infrastructure and transport,
New Zealand Emergency Management and Civil Defense, employment, and other
things. It's a horizontal way of looking at things, and it goes
across the silos. We've got a lot of
strategy and decisions over a silo looking at health
or looking at the ERA reports in education, or we're looking
at
social services. We've not got that nice horizontal look
in how everybody interacts along the way, and that's where some of this conflict is
coming in. So that's where I would like to see us
to start to resolve that. I can see I've got 10 seconds left. Sorry.
No, thank you. Just on that last point, actually, because I think these
people wanting sort of an integrated approach, and I understand that, but this
is about disability support services, which is not education,
it's not housing. It's not these other services which have their own
discrete funding
pots.
They do, but they create
conflict and silos when you look at only disability
support services,
but you're not looking at how they're getting to school.
Or you're not looking at how this impacts the entire
family who are now out of the workforce, for example,
and needing to seek benefits because they're taking care of somebody.
And this interaction all the way across
actually has quite a wide
Impact on a lot of different people.
So it is worth checking that to look for
discrepancies, look at
funding holes, and you're not going to get that view
unless you do those horizontal checks.
It's just a matrix way of doing policy and
legislation checks to make sure that you are being consistent and you
are covering those holes so that you can make really good,
informed decisions, whether it be at budget
time or whether or not it's your policy decision.
If you're not doing those horizontal checks, you're not
looking for the holes and figuring out how to plan to
resolve them. And that's what-
We're well over time.
So I've got the point now. Thank you.
Appreciate you coming to make submissions this afternoon.
Yes, thank you.
All right, our next submitter is Tania in
person.
I think we've got a couple of us preparing.
Was I
right?
Good afternoon. Which one is Tania?
Ah.
I go up and tell you.
Welcome. We have five minutes with you this afternoon.
The time is yours when you're ready.
Kia ora koutou.
I'm here today as a friend and ally of all our
disabilities communities in Aotearoa.
I'm joined by my friend and colleague Juliana and Vera.
It's ironic that I'm the one that gets to speak at this process
because the communities that are affected the most by this bill have
been silenced in this very undemocratic process.
It's really quite shameful.
I was just listening and I'm off script now, but
the speed at which this has been done reminds me of a quote that we
often use in the community.
This is important, so slow down.
Let's really think about that. What's the point of rushing this?
And the other thing I have to wonder about
is why are so many coming to submit.
Something must be wrong.
No matter what Minister Upston tries to tell us or to make us believe,
this bill will change things. It opens up the
loopholes.
So to make things really simple and clear for you, we're going to do a
little role play.
Vera is representing Juliana's family
system.
Vera has an already full life. Vera has
work, whanau commitments, bills to pay,
cleaning and cooking,
and let's try and squeeze in a bit of social life.
Along comes this bill,
shifting the burden. All the things that Juliana
needs,
transport, getting to medical appointments.
Enough.
Enough.
Enough. This bill rips away our rights.
This bill, you know it's going to do?
It's going to destroy my family quality of
life.
This bill is going to destroy as well my
independence.
And this bill is going to destroy my dignity.
And I'm not being alarmist here, but when I
commit suicide because this bill will make my life so
miserable that that's the only option that I have left,
the blood is on your hands.
And when desperate moms who are exhausted to the bone
kill their disabled children because they can no longer take it,
is their blood in your hands?
But the story doesn't end here.
And there is one thing that this bill cannot take away from
us, and that's our hope.
And I have a message for everyone watching.
History teaches that rights are never handed
down by those with power to those who are oppressed.
Rights are claimed. Women in Aotearoa organized and claimed their
rights to vote. Black communities America organized and claimed their civil
rights. And right now in New Zealand, we are organizing, and you're
giving us a very good push to do that. We are organizing.
We are coming together. We are building power.
And I invite you all listening to this oral submission, visit
the DPA website, learn about finding common ground.
Together the National Leadership for Enabling Good Lives, Te Ao Marama
Aotearoa, Disabled Persons Assembly, we are inviting all the
community to come together and shape a declaration for disability rights in
Aotearoa. This is way more than a document.
This is a tool for us to organize, to unite, and to claim our
rights for us by us. Nothing
about us without us.
Honestly,
let's do the right thing.
Kill the bill.
And the other thing is your maths are so wrong.
You really think you're saving money?
This narrative is so harmful.
And finally, my last message is
they're trying to make us powerless, but
we can vote.
So make sure you enroll,
and let's crip the vote.
Thank you so much.
All right. Our next submitter, Foundation for Equity and Research New Zealand in
person.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Oh,
okay. Cool.
Your family.
Oh.
I think we just want to have
another camera.
Right.
Good afternoon and welcome.
Good afternoon.
Pam, Dr Pam, we've got you online. Can you just make
sure you can hear us
okay?
Yes. Absolutely.
Now, just your camera, if you wanted us to see your face, we can't quite see it at
the moment.
I'm blind, so you might want to give me a wee clue.
Oh, yeah. Just tip the camera upwards a bit.
Yeah, there we go.
Perfect.
Perfect. Thank you.
Thank you.
Thanks so much.
All right.
So look, we've got time with you this afternoon.
So look, welcome and thanks for coming along and making a submission.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Kia ora. My name is Pip McLean.
I hold the disability rights portfolio for the Foundation for Equity and Research
New Zealand, or FERNZ for short.
Joining me today on Zoom is Dr Pam McNeil, who is a member of our Hutt
Valley Disability Focus Community Action Group, and sitting next to me is
Mr Mark Drews, who is co-chair of our Kapiti Disability
Community Action Group.
Today we're speaking on behalf of the FERNZ disability-focused Wellington
Regional Community Action groups.
We'd like to acknowledge that you've asked us to address three questions, and we're
very happy to do that.
Thank you.
The first question you have asked is what changes would you like to see to the
bill? Pam.
The bill must be amended to align with enabling good lives
principles and New Zealand's obligations under the
United Nations Convention on Rights for Persons with Disabilities.
Mark.
Specifically,
pause the bill and undertake a
genuine
consultation and co-design with
disabled people
and remove Clause 8 and
11.3 so that support is
not dependent on family
availability and income
or asset testing.
The second question you've asked us is what is your biggest concern? Pam.
The bill moves disability support services away from a
rights-based model
to a system where decisions are made about us
without us.
Mark.
No real consultation has been
given.
Unfair burdens on
families
and the financial risk and
account taken
of the costs of
disability.
Pam.
The overall concern is that the bill attempts to
infantilize disabled people,
removing our right to agency over our own lives.
Pip.
The third question you've asked is what would your solution
be? Pam.
Genuinely co-design, not merely consult
with disabled people.
Ensure the legislation is grounded in enabling good
lives principles and human rights
obligations. Mark.
The solution,
sorry.
This would include pausing the bill and
redesigning a thorough,
genuine co-design process
led by disabled people,
and ensuring
support is based on
disability-related
need, not family availability,
income, savings, or assets.
Pip.
So in summary: pause the bill,
remove Clauses 8 and 11.3, embed choice and
control protections, and require genuine co-design and
consultation with disabled people before any decisions affecting
disability support are made. Thank you for listening to us.
Oh, thank you very much.
I might just leap in with a question I had in my head
first. I'm wondering,
and I appreciate
the consultation
issues that have been raised. But for example, just around Section 8, it says,
"Family support where appropriate." So obviously, there will
need to be an assessment made about whether it's appropriate to rely on family
support or not. I just haven't heard anyone address that point, because
I think there's been an assumption that-
I'd like to do that
... support.
Sorry, what was that, Dr-
Can I do that? So where appropriate,
who's making the decision where appropriate?
I think what happens with a lot of disabled people is that through our lives,
decisions are made about us without us.
Decisions are made over our lives.
We have no agency, and we fight for that along with our
colleagues and our peers as teenagers and young adults.
And then when we finally get that, we want to keep it.
We don't want other people telling us what's appropriate for us.
But unfortunately, that continues to happen.
And whether your view on what's appropriate family support and my view
are probably going to be diametrically opposed.
Sure. All right. No, thanks for addressing that, Dr Pam. Appreciate it.
Look, thank you to all three of you.
Appreciate you coming and making a submission this afternoon.
Thank you for your time.
Thank you.
Joseph. Thank you. We're up. Who's your friend?
Brian.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Please ignore her.
Oh, no. We'd love to see her. What's her name?
If you want to share, you don't have to.
I beg your pardon?
Oh, just wondering what the name of your friend is.
Brian.
Brian.
Oh, cheers. Well, lovely to have you here, Joseph.
Thanks for coming and making a submission this afternoon.
The five minutes with you when you're ready.
Thank you very much. Tēnā koutou katoa.
Ko Joseph toku ingoa. I
go by Joe. Thank you for allowing me time
to speak on this very clement Wellington day.
For context, I was
diagnosed, along with my brother and sister,
with Friedreich's ataxia in 2008.
Since I must be very brief, I'll simply say that it is
a rare inherited genetic disorder
that causes progressive damage to the nervous
system.
From the start, I suggest that you either
delete the references to secondary legislation
or scrap
this pretend legislative framework entirely.
A true framework must clearly state our
rightful supports rather than leaving
the substance to unspecified regulations at the
discretion of future ministers. You cannot
have both a robust framework
and the flexibility to chop and change on a
whim.
This bill does the latter while pretending to
be the former.
In simple terms, don't urinate on my
leg and call it rain.
The minister admitted that she's moving quickly because of the
Supreme Court case,
which forced this bill off slower track.
I'm sure she knows, as you do, that
passing retroactive legislation to
spare the Crown's blushes and purse is
dishonorable.
Doing this for the fifth time this
term erodes public respect for the
rule of law,
and perhaps that shame is why it

As a result, I eventually lost nine toes.
Although my support mainly comes through ACC, I know that the disability
policy affects all disabled people.
It affects our whanau, our communities, and the generations that are yet to
come.
Even after almost 50 years, I am still living with the consequences of these
injuries.
My biggest concern about the bill is the direction it may take.
To me, it feels like disabled people will have to keep proving themselves
over and over again to get the support we need.
Living with disability is already exhausting.
We live with pain, fatigue, surgeries, appointments, and barriers that many
people never see. We shouldn't also have to fight the
very system that is supposed to support us.
For me, disability isn't just about my feet.
It affects how I walk, how long I can stand, my mobility, my
independence,
my mental wellbeing, and my ability to participate in everyday
life,
and my ability to be a part of my community.
I've had multiple surgeries throughout my life, and I'm still having surgeries
today. My most recent surgery was only a couple of months ago, and I'm due to have
more surgery next week.
What should be recognized is that support needs can change over time.
As we age, our bodies change,
our conditions change, and the support we need can change, too.
Another concern I have is that disability
cannot always be reduced to a diagnosis,
a code, or a checklist.
Different doctors and specialists can have different
opinions, so
to describe the same condition in
different ways. If access to support depends too heavily
on a diagnostic label or ticking boxes, there is a real
risk that disabled people won't receive the support they actually need.
Support should be based on the whole person, how disability
affects everyday life, and what will enable us to live independently,
participate in our communities, and contribute to society,
not just on what is written on a medical record.
The hardest part has often not been my disability itself, it's been
navigating systems where I feel like I have to keep
explaining, justifying, and proving my needs every time they change.
That takes a huge emotional toll.
I worry that this bill will make that even harder for many disabled people.
As Māori, disability doesn't just affect one person, it affects the whole
whānau.
Whānau will always support each other because that is what we do.
But support from whānau should be a choice, not an
expectation, because services aren't there.
Disabled people should be able to rely on the services that are designed
to support us, not solely on our families to fill the gaps.
I know from my own journey that getting support isn't just about whether it exists,
it's about whether people can actually access it when they need it.
There is little point having support available on paper if people can't
access it because
of where they live, long waiting lists, transport barriers,
or complicated processes.
When support is difficult to access, the pressure doesn't disappear, it
simply shifts
onto parents, partners, children, siblings, and carers.
That places enormous emotional, physical, and financial pressure on
whānau.
Good disability support doesn't replace whānau, it strengthens them.
It allows disabled people to live with greater
independence while allowing our whānau to be our whānau,
not our only support system.
My request to the committee is please listen to disabled people.
Design a system that recognizes that our needs change over time, and that
support should reflect our lives, not just a diagnosis or a
checklist. Make support accessible,
timely, consistent, and based on trust and
dignity so disabled people can rely on it when they need
it.
